planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; space 6–8 inches apart for ground cover use — Chocolate Chip fills in quickly through stolons and will create a dense mat within 1–2 seasons.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Chocolate Chip is one of the most distinctive and smallest ajuga cultivars — an extremely compact, low-growing selection reaching only 2–4 inches in height (less than half the height of standard ajuga cultivars) with tiny, dark chocolate-brown to bronze-purple leaves that form a miniature, very dense, tight mat of foliage. The diminutive scale makes Chocolate Chip useful in situations where even standard dwarf ajuga would be too tall — between stepping stones, in tight rock garden crevices, as a living carpet under small specimens, and in the smallest garden spaces. In spring, short, upright spikes of blue-violet flowers appear, providing seasonal nectar for early bees and pollinators. The spreading, stoloniferous habit fills in quickly — Chocolate Chip will cover 6–12 inches within the first season of planting and gradually expand to a continuous mat. Unlike some larger ajuga cultivars that can spread aggressively into lawn areas, Chocolate Chip's fine texture and compact stature make it more containable. The dark bronze-purple leaf color provides year-round ornamental interest — the darkest and most interesting foliage color of any common ajuga cultivar. Excellent under ornamental trees, in woodland paths, and as a living ground cover between stepping stones.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs 8–12 inches apart in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Burgundy Glow develops its best tricolor coloration in partial shade with some morning sun — in deep shade the foliage may revert toward plain green.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Burgundy Glow is the most striking variegated ajuga cultivar — a tricolor selection producing leaves in a complex combination of green, creamy white, and deep burgundy-pink to rose-red in irregular patches and blotches across each leaf. The variable, irregular color pattern gives each leaf a unique, painterly appearance, and the overall effect of a mass planting is one of the most dramatic and colorful ground covers available for partial to full shade conditions. The tricolor foliage provides year-round ornamental interest — particularly striking in late winter and spring when the color is most intense — and combines beautifully with solid-foliage companions like hostas, ferns, and dark-leafed heucheras. In spring, short spikes of blue flowers add additional color above the tricolor carpet. The spreading, stoloniferous habit fills in to create a dense mat at 4–6 inches tall. Like all ajugas, Burgundy Glow spreads by runners and may eventually send shoots into adjacent lawn areas — edging the planting area with a physical root barrier or regular edge-trimming keeps the colony contained. Crown rot can affect ajuga in hot, humid conditions with poor air circulation; adequate spacing and well-drained soil prevent this problem in zone 9.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Walker's Low requires excellent drainage and lean soil — in rich, moist soil the stems become floppy and the plant loses its characteristic compact mounding form.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Walker's Low is the most celebrated catmint cultivar — named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 2007 and consistently one of the top-rated perennials in American horticulture for its outstanding combination of compact mounding habit, long bloom season, aromatic silver-gray foliage, and abundant lavender-blue flower production. Despite the name 'Low,' Walker's Low is actually a relatively large catmint reaching 18–24 inches tall with a 24–36 inch spread — the name refers to the garden in Northern Ireland where it was discovered. The characteristic mounding habit, covered from late May through July in zone 9 with dense spikes of lavender-blue flowers, creates one of the most effective mass-planting perennials available for full-sun, dry borders. After the main bloom flush, cut the entire plant back by half — this stimulates vigorous fresh growth and typically produces a substantial second bloom in late summer. The aromatic, silver-gray, textured foliage is attractive when not in flower. Walker's Low is extremely drought-tolerant, deer-resistant, and essentially maintenance-free in well-drained soil. In zone 9, it performs best in zones 4–8 — in the hottest parts of zone 9, summer heat and humidity can stress the plant, but with excellent drainage and lean soil it often persists successfully.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 2007

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Bamboo palm is one of the most cold-sensitive chamaedorea palms — protect from temperatures below 32°F, which can cause significant damage to the foliage and growing points.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Bamboo palm is a multi-stemmed, clumping, shade-tolerant tropical palm native to Mexico and Central America — producing slender, cane-like stems with narrow, pinnately divided, deep-green fronds that create a lush, tropical, bamboo-like effect in shade gardens and interiors. The slender stems, dense clustering habit, and finely divided fronds make it one of the most elegant tropical palms for shaded outdoor gardens in zone 10–12 and one of the finest indoor palms for zone 9 and colder regions. As a landscape plant in zone 10, Bamboo palm creates outstanding screening, privacy hedges, and tropical atmosphere in partial to full shade under trees and near buildings. The palm is exceptionally tolerant of low light — one of the most shade-tolerant palms in cultivation — and maintains attractive appearance in conditions too dark for most other landscape plants. In zone 9, it can be grown outdoors with protection from cold snaps and is excellent as a large container plant moved indoors during winter. It adapts well to indoor conditions — moderate light, regular watering, and good drainage produce vigorous indoor specimens. Provide regular balanced fertilizer during the growing season; avoid over-fertilizing, which can cause salt buildup in container culture.

common_name: Little Spire Russian Sage (Compact Russian Sage)

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; Russian sage requires excellent drainage — it will not survive in heavy clay or wet soil, and is particularly susceptible to crown rot in zone 9's summer humidity if planted in poorly drained sites.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Little Spire is a compact Russian sage cultivar that stays 18–30 inches — roughly half the height of standard Russian sage — making it manageable in typical residential borders where the full-size species (3–5 feet) would require staking and frequent division to remain attractive. The characteristic soft, lavender-blue, hazy flower spikes and silver-white, finely cut aromatic foliage are present in the compact form, creating the same misty, blue-silver cloud effect but in a more contained, neater habit. Russian sage is named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 1995 and remains one of the finest blue-silver perennials for full-sun, dry situations. The hazy lavender-blue flower spikes provide weeks of color from midsummer through fall, and the silver-white stems remain attractive through winter as a structural element in the garden. Strongly deer-resistant; exceptionally drought-tolerant once established; attracts bees and butterflies. The key challenge in zone 9 is the combination of summer heat and humidity — Russian sage succeeds with absolutely excellent drainage, lean soil, and good air circulation. In heavy clay or wet sites, it is short-lived or fails to establish. In well-drained, raised bed conditions or gravelly soils, it is reliably perennial in zone 9.
